problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem C (data type): the Wordle problem. Teams were given a NYT Wordle dataset (daily contest number, solution word, number of reported results, percentage in Hard Mode, and the (1,2,3,4,5,6,X)-try distribution). Four tasks: (1) model daily variation in the number of reported results and give a prediction interval for March 1, 2023, plus assess whether word attributes affect the Hard Mode percentage; (2) predict the (1..6,X) try-distribution for a specific future word (EERIE) on March 1, 2023, with uncertainty; (3) build a classification model of words by difficulty and describe each class's attributes (classify EERIE); (4) report other interesting features of the dataset. This is paper 2318982, an Outstanding (O-award) winner.
models_used: ARIMA(1,1,1) time-series model for number-of-reported-results, with order selected by AICc via the Hyndman-Khandakar stepwise auto.arima algorithm
ADF (Augmented Dickey-Fuller) stationarity test + first-order differencing; Box-Ljung white-noise test for series and residuals
Multiple linear regression (Hard Mode % ~ word attributes), with Pearson correlation screening and LASSO for variable selection; reduced to n_repeat + frequency, R2_adj=0.782
Regression diagnostics: residual normality (Q-Q), homoscedasticity, linearity, and VIF for multicollinearity

Absorbing Markov chain over a reduced state space (n_green, n_yellow, n_grey) + failure state X, 384 states, to model the guessing process and derive the first-passage / absorption-time distribution = the (1..6,X) try distribution

Information-theoretic transfer probabilities: Shannon information of a state via -log2(card of compatible words / card(W)); softmax/Boltzmann transition kernel exp(k(I_j - I_i)+C) over reachable states
Two player strategies: 'probability-first' (sigmoid of word frequency as subjective answer probability) and 'information-first' (expected information gain E[I_x] of the opening word)
Cross-entropy (MLE-style) fit to recover the daily mix proportion q1(r) of the two strategies from observed distributions
Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) to forecast the future strategy-mix proportion q1 for March 1, 2023 (point 0.63, 95% CI [0.59,0.67])
K-Prototypes clustering (handles mixed numeric + categorical word attributes) into 3 difficulty classes; difficulty score as weighted sum of try-percentages with X weighted as 8
Kullback-Leibler divergence between class difficulty-score densities as a cluster-separation / accuracy metric
key_techniques: NLP feature engineering on the solution word: POS class via NLTK nltk.pos_tag (collapsed to NN/RB/JJ/Other), syllable count via textstat, polarity and subjectivity via TextBlob sentiment, letter-repetition count, and corpus frequency from the Kaggle 'English Word Frequency' / Google Trillion-Word corpus

Reframing a sequential game as an absorbing Markov chain and equating the answer (try distribution) to the absorption-time distribution computed via Hadamard-product iterations of the transition matrix with E = 11^T - I

Dramatic state-space reduction: collapsing 5x26 green/yellow + 26 grey letter-position states down to a 3-tuple count vector, with explicit reachability set A_i constraints derived from game rules (greens never lost, greens+yellows non-decreasing, grey non-decreasing)

Using information entropy both for the opening-word choice (E[I_x]) and for state value I_i to drive a single physically-motivated Boltzmann transition kernel, with the strategy index k entering the exponent so one formula covers both strategies
Estimating an unobservable latent (per-day strategy mix q1) by minimizing cross-entropy between the model-implied and observed try distributions, then forecasting that latent series with GPR
AICc-minimizing stepwise model selection rather than eyeballing ACF/PACF (paper explicitly notes ACF/PACF reading is 'subjective' and defers to the algorithm)
Data cleaning of documented dataset errors: fixing non-5-letter words (rprobe->probe, clen->clean, tash->trash) and the 2569->25569 count typo, using COMAP's own erratum note plus external lookup
Sensitivity analysis by sweeping the free transition constant C in {1,2,3} and showing q1 forecast barely moves
why_it_won: The paper's signature is Task 2: instead of a black-box regression on past distributions, it derives the (1..6,X) distribution mechanistically as the absorption-time distribution of an absorbing Markov chain whose transitions are governed by Shannon information gain. This is a genuinely original, theory-grounded construction that judges reward — it explains WHY the distribution looks as it does rather than merely fitting it. It is layered cleverly: two interpretable player strategies, a latent daily strategy-mix q1 recovered by cross-entropy, and GPR to forecast that latent into the future, so the final EERIE prediction is the composition of several defensible sub-models rather than one fragile fit. Every component is a recognized, interpretable method (ARIMA via AICc, LASSO-reduced regression with full diagnostics + VIF, K-Prototypes for mixed data, KL divergence as a validation metric), so the work reads as rigorous AND novel — the exact combination judges look for. It also closes the loop: EERIE is independently classified 'hard' by the clustering model and the Task-2 Markov chain shows mass shifted toward 4+ tries, giving cross-method consistency. Engineering hygiene (documented data fixes, stationarity/white-noise tests, residual diagnostics, sensitivity sweep on C) plus a clean keyword list and a warm, on-brief Letter to the Puzzle Editor round out an Outstanding-caliber submission.
section_highlights: Summary is dense and sequential — it walks all four tasks and even states the final EERIE distribution (0.00,0.15,11.05,28.44,35.46,21.16,3.76), so a judge sees the whole arc up front. The flowchart (Figure 1) and a dedicated Markov-chain flowchart (Figure 12) make the multi-stage Task-2 pipeline legible. Assumptions (2.1) are each paired with justification and are actually load-bearing — assumption (5) 'higher information gain => lower probability' directly motivates the transition kernel, and (1)/(3) (rational, greedy, never discard info) justify the Markov property and the reachability constraints. Notation table (2.2) is thorough. Task 1 documents real ADF statistics (-2.895 p=0.199 before diff; -5.065 p=0.01 after) and Box-Ljung values, and honestly notes ACF/PACF reading is subjective before deferring to AICc. Regression section shows the full model, then the LASSO-reduced model with R2_adj=0.782 and a complete diagnostic battery + VIF table. The interpretation of signs (frequency positive, n_repeat negative on Hard Mode %) is given a behavioral story via Twitter/community information. Task 4 surfaces concrete findings: hardest word 'parer' (0.48 at 7+ tries), volatility dates tied to easy/hard words (drink up, dodge down), first-guess letter-frequency analysis, and a sharp data-integrity observation that >2% first-try success on 'poise' implies misrepresentation/answer leakage. The Letter to the Puzzle Editor is genuinely audience-appropriate and warm.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The Markov state-space reduction to (n_green, n_yellow, n_grey) discards letter/position identity, so the transition probabilities are population-averaged over all words; the model cannot truly be word-specific except through the day-r initial distribution and the global I_i estimate — the EERIE-specific prediction leans heavily on the initial distribution, not on EERIE-specific dynamics

The transition kernel exp(k(I_j-I_i)+C) is admittedly ad hoc; the paper itself concedes 'a certain degree of arbitrariness'. The constant C is a free parameter justified only by a 3-point sensitivity sweep, and the strategy index k entering linearly in the exponent is unmotivated beyond convenience

Subjective-probability via sigmoid p_x = 1/(1+e^{-(freq_x - k)}) with k = median frequency is heuristic; word frequencies span orders of magnitude so this likely saturates and is sensitive to the frequency corpus chosen

ARIMA(1,1,1) on a sharply decaying, novelty-driven series extrapolated 60 days out is fragile; the wide 90% PI [10517,27007] around 16529 reflects that, and a differenced near-random-walk gives limited real predictive signal

Multiple-regression caveat the authors flag themselves: Hard Mode is chosen before seeing the word, so any correlation with word attributes must be mediated by community/Twitter spoilers — a causal story asserted, not tested

K-Prototypes fixed at 3 clusters with no reported elbow/silhouette selection; KL divergence is used as an 'accuracy' proxy but it measures class separation, not classification correctness against ground-truth difficulty (there is no labeled difficulty target)

Several rendered figures/tables in the markdown are clearly OCR-garbled (e.g., the 'love'-spam cluster image, placeholder ACF=1.00 tables), so some quantitative plots can't be independently verified from the artifact
Assumption (4) that all players share one ~36k-word bank and identical subjective probabilities is strong and ignores skill heterogeneity, which is exactly what drives the try distribution's spread
reusable_patterns: For any 'predict a discrete outcome distribution' data task, consider modeling the generating PROCESS as an absorbing Markov chain and reading the answer off the absorption-time distribution — far more defensible to judges than regressing distributions directly

Drive Markov/agent transition probabilities with an information-theoretic potential (Shannon entropy / information gain) wrapped in a Boltzmann/softmax kernel exp(beta*(I_j-I_i)) — gives a principled, single-formula transition model with one tunable temperature

When a key driver is latent (here, the per-day mix of two player strategies), recover it by fitting model-implied to observed distributions via cross-entropy/MLE, then forecast that recovered latent series with a flexible regressor (GPR) to push predictions into the future — a clean two-stage 'estimate-then-forecast' pattern
Always pick ARIMA order by AICc-minimizing stepwise search (Hyndman-Khandakar / auto.arima) and explicitly say ACF/PACF reading is subjective — shows methodological maturity
For mixed numeric+categorical features, use K-Prototypes (not k-means) and validate cluster separation with KL divergence between per-class outcome densities

Engineer rich NLP features cheaply from off-the-shelf libraries: NLTK POS tags, textstat syllables, TextBlob polarity/subjectivity, plus an external frequency corpus — turns a bare word column into a regression/clustering-ready feature matrix
Reduce a combinatorial state space to a small count-vector summary and derive explicit reachability constraints from the problem's rules to keep the model tractable (384 states here)

Open the Summary by stating the final numeric answer, give per-assumption justification, run the standard test battery (stationarity, white-noise, residual diagnostics, VIF), add a sensitivity sweep over the one free parameter, and cross-validate the headline answer with a second independent model (clustering agreeing with the Markov chain on EERIE = hard)
Do explicit, documented data cleaning of dataset errata (typos, malformed rows) early and cite the source of each fix — signals diligence to judges
